title: Self-Employed Freelancer: Variable Income and Tax Strategy
constraints: No guaranteed income months during client gaps
key_risks: Income disruption with no disability coverage
No health insurance — catastrophic financial exposure
Retirement savings severely underfunded vs. age
Tax underpayment risk without estimated quarterly payments

planning_issues.description: No quarterly estimated tax payments; self-employment tax 15.3%; Solo 401k and SEP-IRA can dramatically reduce taxable income
No health insurance; own-occupation disability missing; income fully exposed to health event
$18,000 at age 35 on $95K income is severely underfunded; Solo 401k allows up to $46,000/year in contributions
Irregular income requires 6-month buffer and percentage-based savings approach rather than fixed amounts

key_recommendations: Open Solo 401k immediately; contribute maximum employee + employer portions
Set up quarterly estimated tax payments to avoid IRS penalties
Enroll in ACA marketplace health insurance at next open enrollment
Add own-occupation disability insurance (2-3% of income/month)
Build 6-month cash buffer before aggressively investing
follow_up_questions: Is Michael operating as sole proprietor or LLC/S-Corp? (S-Corp may reduce SE tax above ~$60K)
What is the client contract duration — project-based or retainer?
Does Michael track all deductible business expenses (home office, equipment, travel)?
Has an S-Corp election been evaluated to reduce SE tax on distributions?
outcome_summary: Solo 401k opened with $38,000 contributed in year one. ACA health plan enrolled. Quarterly tax payments established. S-Corp election evaluated for following year.
key_lesson: Self-employed individuals must function as their own HR department — Solo 401k, SEP-IRA, and S-Corp structuring can reduce tax burden by $8,000–$15,000/year while simultaneously building retirement savings.
